sections.name: Foundation
sections.summary: Core Git mechanics — init, staging, commits, undo, and history inspection.

sections.name: Branching Workflows
sections.summary: Branching, switching, merging, hotfix and release workflows.

sections.name: History & Collaboration
sections.summary: Rebase, conflict resolution, recovery, remotes, and PR lifecycle.

1.title: Repo Kickoff
1.focus: Initialize repository and inspect working tree
concepts: A repository is a folder tracked by Git
git init creates a .git directory that stores all version history
git status shows tracked, untracked, and staged files
git add stages files from working directory to staging area
The staging area is a buffer between your edits and the next commit
commonMistakes: Running git init inside an existing repo (nested repos)
Forgetting to stage files before committing
Not understanding the difference between working directory and staging area
realWorldScenarios: Starting a new project from scratch
Converting an existing folder into a Git repo
Checking what files are ready to commit
1.successCriteria: Student can initialize a repo, check status, and stage files confidently

2.title: Commit Hygiene
concepts: A commit is a snapshot of your staged changes with a message
git commit -m creates a commit with an inline message
Commit messages should be short, imperative, and descriptive
git log --oneline shows compact commit history
Each commit has a unique SHA hash
commonMistakes: Writing vague commit messages like 'update' or 'fix'
Committing too many changes in one commit
Not checking status before committing
realWorldScenarios: Saving progress on a feature before taking a break
Creating atomic commits for easy code review
Reviewing what was done yesterday via log
2.successCriteria: Student writes clean commits with good messages and reads history

3.title: Staging Mastery
concepts: git restore discards changes in working directory
git restore --staged unstages files without losing edits
You can selectively stage files instead of using git add .
The staging area lets you craft commits precisely
commonMistakes: Using git restore when they meant git restore --staged
Accidentally discarding changes they wanted to keep
Not understanding the difference between unstaging and discarding
realWorldScenarios: Accidentally editing the wrong file and need to undo
Staged too many files and need to unstage some
Experimenting with code and deciding to throw it away
3.successCriteria: Student can stage, unstage, and discard changes deliberately

4.title: Debug with Log
4.focus: Investigate changes with log and diff
concepts: git diff shows line-by-line changes between working directory and staging
git diff --staged shows what's about to be committed
git log can be filtered by author, date, file, and search string
Reading diffs is a core debugging skill
commonMistakes: Confusing git diff (unstaged) with git diff --staged
Not reading the full diff before committing
Ignoring the context lines in diffs
realWorldScenarios: Reviewing your own changes before pushing
Finding when a bug was introduced by reading log
Checking what changed in a specific file
4.successCriteria: Student can inspect changes and history to understand what happened

5.title: Safe Undo
concepts: git reset --soft HEAD~1 undoes the last commit but keeps changes staged
git reset --mixed HEAD~1 undoes commit and unstages (default reset)
git restore discards working directory changes
These are safe local operations that don't affect remote
commonMistakes: Using git reset --hard when they meant --soft (loses changes!)
Confusing reset with revert (reset rewrites history, revert creates new commit)
Not understanding HEAD~1 notation
realWorldScenarios: Committed with a typo in the message
Committed the wrong files and need to redo
Want to squash the last few commits into one
5.successCriteria: Student can undo commits and changes without losing work

6.title: Team-Ready Basics
concepts: Atomic commits contain one logical change each
Commit messages follow conventions: imperative mood, 50 char subject
Clean history makes code review and debugging easier
Reviewing your own log before pushing catches mistakes
commonMistakes: Mixing unrelated changes in one commit
Not reviewing staged changes before committing
Pushing without checking history first
realWorldScenarios: Preparing a feature branch for team review
Writing commits a teammate can understand 6 months later
Cleaning up messy local history before sharing
6.successCriteria: Student produces professional-quality commits ready for team collaboration

7.title: Feature Branch Start
concepts: Branches are lightweight pointers to commits
git checkout -b creates and switches to a new branch in one step
Feature branches isolate work from main
Each branch has its own commit history diverging from the branch point
commonMistakes: Creating a branch but forgetting to switch to it
Working on main instead of the feature branch
Not committing before switching branches
realWorldScenarios: Starting work on a new feature without breaking the stable codebase
Isolating experimental changes from production code
Working on a bug fix while a feature is in progress
7.successCriteria: Student can create, switch to, and commit on a feature branch

8.title: Parallel Workstreams
concepts: You can have multiple branches active simultaneously
git checkout switches between existing branches
Always commit or stash before switching to avoid conflicts
git branch lists all local branches
commonMistakes: Switching branches with uncommitted changes
Losing track of which branch you're on
Creating duplicate branches by accident
realWorldScenarios: Switching from a feature to fix an urgent bug, then switching back
Managing work across multiple tickets simultaneously
Reviewing a colleague's branch locally
8.successCriteria: Student can juggle multiple branches cleanly without losing work

9.title: Fast-Forward Merge
concepts: A fast-forward merge moves the branch pointer forward without a merge commit
Fast-forward happens when the target branch has no new commits since branching
git merge integrates one branch's changes into the current branch
After merging, the feature branch can be safely deleted
commonMistakes: Merging from the wrong branch direction
Forgetting to switch to main before merging
Not checking log after merge to verify the result
realWorldScenarios: Merging a completed feature back into main
Integrating a quick hotfix developed in isolation
Cleaning up after a successful code review
9.successCriteria: Student understands and can perform fast-forward merges correctly

10.title: Release Branch Drill
concepts: Hotfix branches branch from main for urgent production fixes
Release branches stabilize code before deployment
Hotfixes get merged back to both main and develop
Branch naming conventions: hotfix/*, release/*, feature/*
commonMistakes: Branching hotfix from develop instead of main
Forgetting to merge hotfix back into develop
Mixing feature work into a hotfix branch
realWorldScenarios: Production is broken — ship a fix in 30 minutes
Preparing a release while feature development continues
Managing parallel release and hotfix timelines
10.successCriteria: Student can execute hotfix and release branch workflows

11.title: Branch Policy
concepts: Branch naming conventions make repos navigable (feature/, bugfix/, hotfix/)
Protected branches prevent direct pushes to main
Review-ready branches have clean, atomic commits
Squashing messy commits before review improves readability
commonMistakes: Inconsistent branch naming across a team
Pushing directly to main instead of using feature branches
Leaving debug commits in a review branch
realWorldScenarios: Setting up branch protection rules for a team repo
Preparing a branch for pull request review
Enforcing naming conventions in a CI pipeline

12.title: Integration Readiness
concepts: Always pull latest main before merging to avoid conflicts
Test your branch against latest main before creating a PR
git status after merge confirms clean state
Integration testing catches issues that unit tests miss
commonMistakes: Merging into an outdated main branch
Not testing after merge to catch integration issues
Ignoring merge conflicts and hoping for the best
realWorldScenarios: Syncing your feature branch with latest main before review
Resolving issues found during integration testing
Final checks before a deployment merge
12.successCriteria: Student can verify integration readiness and merge confidently

13.title: Rebase Control
concepts: Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch's latest commit
Produces linear history without merge commits
git rebase main while on feature branch moves feature commits ahead
Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch
commonMistakes: Rebasing a public/shared branch (rewrites history others depend on)
Confusing rebase with merge — rebase rewrites, merge preserves
Not understanding that rebase changes commit SHAs
realWorldScenarios: Cleaning up a feature branch before PR for linear history
Updating a feature branch with latest main without merge commits
Keeping a clean git log for projects that require linear history
13.successCriteria: Student can rebase and understands when NOT to rebase

14.title: Conflict Surgery
concepts: Conflicts happen when two branches modify the same lines
Git marks conflicts with <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers
git status shows which files have conflicts
git merge --abort cancels the merge and returns to pre-merge state
After resolving, git add the files and git commit to complete
commonMistakes: Accepting one side blindly without understanding both changes
Leaving conflict markers in the code
Panicking and using git merge --abort when the conflict is simple
realWorldScenarios: Two developers edited the same config file
Merging a long-running feature branch that diverged significantly
Resolving conflicts during a rebase
14.successCriteria: Student can identify, understand, and resolve merge conflicts

15.title: Recovery Toolkit
concepts: git revert creates a new commit that undoes a previous commit safely
git reflog shows every HEAD movement — your safety net
reflog lets you find lost commits after a bad reset or rebase
revert vs reset: revert is safe for shared history, reset rewrites it
commonMistakes: Using reset on shared branches instead of revert
Not knowing reflog exists when they think commits are lost
Reverting a merge commit without specifying the parent
realWorldScenarios: A bad commit was pushed to main and needs to be undone safely
Accidentally ran git reset --hard and need to recover
Rolling back a deployment without rewriting history
15.successCriteria: Student can recover from mistakes using revert and reflog

16.title: Remote Sync Master
concepts: git fetch downloads remote changes without merging
git pull = git fetch + git merge
git push -u origin sets upstream tracking for the branch
origin is the default name for your remote repository
Tracking branches let git pull/push know where to sync
commonMistakes: Pushing without pulling first (causes rejected push)
Confusing fetch with pull (fetch doesn't change your files)
Not setting upstream with -u on first push
realWorldScenarios: Syncing your local repo with GitHub every morning
Pushing a new feature branch for the first time
Pulling teammate's changes before starting work
16.successCriteria: Student can sync with remote repos using fetch, pull, and push

17.title: PR Command Center
concepts: Pull requests are the standard way to propose changes on GitHub
PRs include description, reviewers, labels, and linked issues
CI checks run automatically on PR branches
Squash merge combines all PR commits into one clean commit on main
Review approval is required before merging in protected repos
commonMistakes: Creating PRs without descriptions
Merging without waiting for CI checks to pass
Not responding to review comments before merging
realWorldScenarios: Creating a PR for a completed feature with description and reviewers
Responding to code review feedback and pushing fixes
Squash merging after approval for clean main history
17.successCriteria: Student can manage the full PR lifecycle from creation to merge

18.title: Git & GitHub King
concepts: Incident response requires fast, clean Git workflows
Fetch latest, create hotfix branch, fix, rebase, PR, squash merge
Every step must be deliberate — production stability depends on it
Combine all skills: branching, rebasing, PRs, and clean history
This is the real-world Git workflow used at production companies
commonMistakes: Panicking and pushing directly to main
Skipping the PR process during an incident
Not rebasing before creating the PR
realWorldScenarios: Production is down — execute the full incident response workflow
Critical security patch needs to ship in under an hour
Coordinating a hotfix with a team under time pressure
18.successCriteria: Student can execute a full incident response Git workflow under pressure
